task: I’m cleaning up a small labeling workflow, and I want to make sure I’m not setting myself up for a stupid mix-up later. Can you help me think through a simple way to organize the main set, the backups, and the notes so nothing gets crossed?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it simple and make the separation obvious.

A clean way to organize it:
- **Main set:** the active, current labels only
- **Backups:** stored separately, clearly marked as backup/spare
- **Notes:** one short control note that records what was used, what was set aside, and any exceptions

To avoid mix-ups:
- give each set a **clear name or ID**
- keep **main and backup physically separate**
- put **date/version info** on every set
- note any **swap, reprint, or correction** right away
- don’t mix working notes into the main set; keep them in the control note or log

A simple rule of thumb:
- if someone needs to use it now, it belongs in the **main set**
- if it’s only there in case of failure, it belongs in the **backup set**
- if it explains what happened, it belongs in the **notes**

If you want, I can turn this into a very short labeling checklist or a 3-column log format.

user_simulator.text:
Close enough, but it’s still a little too generic. I want something I can actually use without thinking twice.

Make it tighter:
- one naming convention for the main set and backups
- one place for the control notes
- a rule for what gets logged immediately vs. what can wait
- and how to keep duplicate labels from ending up in the same folder or bin

I don’t need theory. I need a simple layout, maybe a 3-column log or a folder/bin scheme, that makes it hard to screw up when things are busy.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — use one simple layout and make the “wrong place” hard to reach.

### 1) Naming convention
Use this pattern everywhere:

**`[Project/Case]-[Set]-[Date]-[Version]`**

Examples:
- `A12-MAIN-2026-04-29-v1`
- `A12-BACKUP-2026-04-29-v1`
- `A12-BACKUP-2026-04-29-v2`

Rules:
- **MAIN** = the active set only
- **BACKUP** = spare/duplicate set only
- **Version changes** only when something is reprinted, corrected, or swapped
- Don’t reuse old names for new labels

### 2) One place for control notes
Keep **one control log only** for the whole workflow.

Put it in a single folder or binder called:
**`CONTROL - A12`**

That folder contains:
- what set is current
- what backups exist
- what changed
- who changed it
- when it changed
- why it changed

Nothing else goes there.

### 3) What gets logged immediately vs. later
Log **immediately** if it changes the active set or could cause confusion later:
- reprint
- correction
- swap
- damaged label
- moved from backup to main
- missing or duplicate label found
- any exception

Can wait until the end of the batch if it’s just routine and low-risk:
- normal completion
- count check
- “no issues” note
- filing the finished set

If you’re unsure, **log it now**.

### 4) Keep duplicate labels out of the same folder/bin
Use **separate physical bins** and **separate folder names**:

- **Bin 1: MAIN ONLY**
- **Bin 2: BACKUP ONLY**
- **Bin 3: HOLD / REVIEW**
- **Folder: CONTROL ONLY**

Hard rule:
- no backup goes in the main bin
- no main set goes in the backup bin
- no duplicates sit loose in the same bin “for convenience”

If you find a duplicate:
1. pull it out immediately
2. mark it **DUPLICATE**
3. put it in **HOLD / REVIEW**
4. log it in the control note

### 5) Simple 3-column log
Use this:

| Date/Time | Action | Result / Next Step |
|---|---|---|
| 2026-04-29 09:10 | Printed MAIN v1 | Placed in MAIN bin |
| 2026-04-29 09:15 | Printed BACKUP v1 | Placed in BACKUP bin |
| 2026-04-29 09:40 | Found duplicate label | Moved to HOLD / REVIEW, logged for check |

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page “do not think, just follow” checklist with exact folder/bin labels.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still one hole: the naming is fine, but I’d tighten the physical layout so it’s idiot-proof when I’m busy.

Do it like this:

- **MAIN** = left bin / left folder
- **BACKUP** = right bin / right folder
- **CONTROL** = separate notebook or single file, never mixed with either bin
- **HOLD/REVIEW** = one ugly quarantine spot, not optional

And I’d use only one naming pattern everywhere:

**CASEID - MAIN or BACKUP - YYYYMMDD - v#**

Example:
- `A12-MAIN-20260429-v1`
- `A12-BACKUP-20260429-v1`

Rule for logging:
- if it changes where something lives, or changes which set is active, log it **immediately**
- if it’s just routine count/final closeout, it can wait

Duplicate labels:
- never in the same bin
- if two copies show up, one goes to **HOLD/REVIEW** at once
- control note gets the time, what it was, and where it went

I’d keep the log dead simple:

| Time | Item / Set | Action / Location |
|---|---|---|
| 09:10 | A12 MAIN v1 | Printed, left bin |
| 09:15 | A12 BACKUP v1 | Printed, right bin |
| 09:40 | Duplicate found | Moved to HOLD/REVIEW |

That’s probably close, but I’d still want one more thing: a rule for what happens when MAIN and BACKUP both get touched in the same hour. Otherwise somebody will “just set it aside” and then you’re hunting it later.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — add a **single priority rule** so there’s no hesitation when both sets get touched.

### Add this rule:
**If MAIN and BACKUP are both handled in the same hour, MAIN gets logged first and BACKUP only after MAIN is either placed, corrected, or moved to HOLD/REVIEW.**

### In practice:
- **MAIN touched first** → log it immediately
- **BACKUP touched in the same hour** → log it next, with the exact reason
- If either one is not fully settled, it goes to **HOLD/REVIEW**
- Don’t leave either one “temporarily aside” without logging it

### Simple decision rule:
- **MAIN changed?** log now
- **BACKUP changed?** log now
- **Both changed?** log MAIN first, then BACKUP
- **Not resolved yet?** put it in HOLD/REVIEW and note why

### Tiny add-on for the log:
| Time | Item / Set | Action / Location |
|---|---|---|
| 09:10 | A12 MAIN v1 | Printed, left bin |
| 09:15 | A12 BACKUP v1 | Printed, right bin |
| 09:40 | A12 MAIN v1 | Removed for correction, HOLD/REVIEW |
| 09:42 | A12 BACKUP v1 | Checked after MAIN, stayed right bin |

### The hard rule:
**No unlogged “set aside” state.**
If it’s out of MAIN or BACKUP and not finished, it lives in **HOLD/REVIEW** and gets written down.

If you want, I can fold all of this into one compact SOP block you can paste into a note or binder tab.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That part needs a hard rule, not hand-waving.

Use this:

**If MAIN and BACKUP are both touched in the same hour, whichever one is touched first gets logged first, and the second one gets logged immediately after — no gap, no “I’ll come back to it.”**

If either one is not fully settled, it goes straight to **HOLD/REVIEW**. Not the desk. Not the edge of the bin. Not “temporary.”

I’d write it like this:
- **MAIN touched** → log now
- **BACKUP touched same hour** → log immediately after MAIN
- **Any uncertainty** → HOLD/REVIEW first, then log
- **No unlogged set-aside state**

And if you want it even tighter, add one line to the log template:

| Time | Item / Set | Action / Location | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 09:10 | A12 MAIN v1 | Removed for correction | HOLD/REVIEW |
| 09:12 | A12 BACKUP v1 | Checked, unchanged | RIGHT BIN |

That way nobody has to interpret what “touched” means. If it moved, changed, got checked, or got pulled — it gets written.

If you want, I can compress the whole thing into a one-block SOP with the bins, naming, HOLD spot, and the same-hour rule all in one place.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the tight version, with the same-hour rule baked in.

## Physical layout
- **MAIN** = left bin / left folder
- **BACKUP** = right bin / right folder
- **CONTROL** = separate notebook or single file, never mixed with either bin
- **HOLD/REVIEW** = one dedicated quarantine spot, not optional

## Naming
Use one pattern everywhere:

**`CASEID - MAIN or BACKUP - YYYYMMDD - v#`**

Examples:
- `A12-MAIN-20260429-v1`
- `A12-BACKUP-20260429-v1`

## Logging rule
- If it changes where something lives, or changes which set is active, **log it immediately**
- If it’s just routine count or final closeout, it can wait

## Same-hour rule
**If MAIN and BACKUP are both touched in the same hour, whichever one is touched first gets logged first, and the second one gets logged immediately after — no gap, no “I’ll come back to it.”**

## Duplicate rule
- Never keep duplicates in the same bin
- If two copies show up, one goes straight to **HOLD/REVIEW**
- CONTROL note gets the time, what it was, and where it went

## Log format
Keep it dead simple:

| Time | Item / Set | Action / Location | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 09:10 | A12 MAIN v1 | Removed for correction | HOLD/REVIEW |
| 09:12 | A12 BACKUP v1 | Checked, unchanged | RIGHT BIN |

## Hard rule
**No unlogged set-aside state.**
If it leaves MAIN or BACKUP and isn’t finished, it goes to **HOLD/REVIEW** and gets written down.

If you want, I can turn this into a one-block SOP you can paste directly into your control note.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the whole point — no floating state, no improv.

This is tight enough:

- **MAIN** = left bin / left folder
- **BACKUP** = right bin / right folder
- **CONTROL** = separate notebook or single file, never mixed with either bin
- **HOLD/REVIEW** = one quarantine spot, not optional

**Naming:** `CASEID - MAIN or BACKUP - YYYYMMDD - v#`

**Log now if:**
- it changes where something lives
- it changes which set is active
- it gets pulled, checked, corrected, swapped, or duplicated

**Can wait if:**
- routine count
- final closeout
- no change to location or status

**Same-hour rule:**
- first touch gets logged first
- second touch gets logged immediately after
- no gap, no “I’ll come back to it”

**Duplicate rule:**
- never same bin
- one copy goes straight to HOLD/REVIEW
- CONTROL gets the time, item, and destination

And the log format is fine with the status column. That’s enough to keep people from pretending something was “just set aside.”

I’d use it.
